{{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring revision.comment: Adding [[Category:bumping to try to fix the sexagenary stuff]][[sn:{{subst:PAGENAME}}]][[Category:bumping to try to fix the sexagenary stuff]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine (Nara)|ja|飛鳥神社}} [[ja:幸徳井家]] [[Category:Kōtokui Family]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Kōtokui {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine (Nara)|ja|飛鳥神社}} [[ja:幸徳井家]] [[Category:Kōtokui Family]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Kōtokui The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == Related Topics == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine (Nara)|ja|飛鳥神社}} [[ja:幸徳井家]] [[Category:Kōtokui Family]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:Kōtokui He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * {{ill|Asuka Shrine (Nara)|ja|飛鳥神社}} [[ja:幸徳井家]] [[Category:Kōtokui Family]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kōtokui Family]][[Category:Kōtokui Family]][[Category:Unsorted {{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} * revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=October 2019}} {{Infobox Japanese clan | name = Kōtokui family | crest = Unknown.jpg | crest_caption = | main_house = {{ill|Kamo no Asomi clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} (branch family) | founder = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} | type = {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}}
{{ill|Shizoku|ja|士族}} | origin = {{ill|Yamato Province|ja|大和国}} Kōtokui in Takahata Village | residence = Kōtokui, Takahata Village, Yamato Province
Present-day {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良市}} Takahatachō and Onyōchō | notable_members = {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}} }} '''Kōtokui family''' (幸徳井家) refers to a {{ill|Jige-ke|ja|地下家}} based in {{ill|Nara|ja|奈良}}, engaged in {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} as their hereditary occupation. Though originally descendants of the {{ill|Abe clan (Onmyōdō)|ja|阿倍氏}}, the family became associated with the {{ill|Kamo clan|ja|賀茂朝臣氏}} after {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}}, the founder, was adopted by the Kamo clan. {{see also|Kamo clan|Kadenokōji family (Kamo clan)}} == Overview == In 1391, Tomoyuki was born as the second son of {{ill|Abe no Tomouji|ja|安倍友氏}}, a branch family of the Abe clan. Tomoyuki studied {{ill|Onmyōdō|ja|陰陽道}} under {{ill|Kamo no Sadahiro|ja|賀茂定弘}}, who served as {{ill|Gyōbu-kyō|ja|刑部卿}} and {{ill|Onmyōjō|ja|陰陽助}}. In 1419, Tomoyuki was adopted by Sadahiro and took the name Kamo no Tomoyuki. He rose to the rank of {{ill|Senior Third Rank|ja|正三位|1=Senior Third Rank}} by 1463 and established his residence in Kōtokui, a well-known area near {{ill|Takahata Village|ja|高畠村}}, serving as a political advisor to {{ill|Jinson|ja|尋尊}} of {{ill|Daijōin Temple|ja|大乗院}}. The family name "Kōtokui" derives from this location. After Tomoyuki's death in 1473, his descendants continued to serve Daijōin Temple and played key roles in supervising {{ill|Onmyōji|ja|陰陽師}} and {{ill|Shōmonshi|ja|声聞師}} in the Nara region, even compiling their own calendar, the {{ill|Kōtokui Calendar|ja|幸徳井暦}}. === Edo Period === In the early Edo period, {{ill|Tsuchimikado Yasuhiro|ja|土御門泰重}} resigned from the {{ill|Onmyōryō|ja|陰陽寮}} due to repeated failures, leaving a vacancy for {{ill|Onmyō no Kami|ja|陰陽頭}}. With the main Kamo family line extinct, {{ill|Kōtokui Tomokage|ja|幸徳井友景}}, the ninth head of the Kōtokui family, was appointed Onmyō no Kami in 1618. This appointment led to a series of Kōtokui family members, such as {{ill|Kōtokui Tomotane|ja|幸徳井友種}} and {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoden|ja|幸徳井友傳}}, succeeding to this position. However, by the late 17th century, the {{ill|Tsuchimikado family|ja|土御門家}} regained the title of Onmyō no Kami and stripped the Kōtokui family of their control over southern Nara's Onmyōji. Despite this, the Kōtokui family maintained influence over Nara temples and continued advising on temple rituals. === Meiji Period and Beyond === Following the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the Kōtokui family petitioned to be ennobled as {{ill|Kazoku|ja|華族}} due to their historical contributions. The petition was denied, and their activities after this period remain unknown. == Heads of the Kōtokui Family == 1. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoyuki|ja|幸徳井友幸}} (1391–1473) - Senior Third Rank 2. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomoshige|ja|幸徳井友重}} (1411–1490) - Senior Third Rank 3. {{ill|Kōtokui Tomonobu|ja|幸徳井友延}} (1435–1494) - Junior Third Rank ... 24. {{ill|Kōtokui Yasuhiko|ja|幸徳井保章}} (1847–?) == References in Literature == * {{Cite book|author=Arayama Toru|title=Yagyū Onmyōken|year=2008|isbn=978-4-10-121042-1}} * {{ill|Azano Kōhei|ja|あざの耕平}}, ''Tokyo Ravens'' - A {{ill|Light novel|ja|ライトノベル}} series featuring Onmyōji, including Kōtokui family members in a supporting role. == References == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Chintaku Reifu Shrine|ja|鎮宅霊符神社}} * {{ill|Nara Educational University|ja|奈良教育大学}} *
